3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nivins, S., Sauce, B., Liebherr, M., Judd, N., & Klingberg, T. (2024). Long-term impact of digital media on brain development in children. Scientific Reports, 14, 13030.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Of the 11,875 children from the ABCD study cohort, 6469 children (age, mean (SD) = 9.9 (0.6) years; boys, n (%) = 3369 (52.1%)) fulfilled our inclusion criteria and were included at the T0 visit (i.e., 9-11 years of age). Of these, 4610 children were included at the T2 visit, and 1697 children were included at the T4 visit. 1462 children had usable data for all three-time points.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Individual digital media usage: using social media, playing video games, or watching television/videos (average estimated time spent across time points)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (average hours/day; weighted average of weekday and weekend, averaged across available time points T0-T4)
3C_Exposure.time_frame: Typical weekday and weekend day, averaged across T0 and T4
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The estimated time spent on individual DM usage (i.e., using social media, playing video games, or watching television/videos) was assessed at all annual visits using the self-reported Youth Screen Time Survey. To calculate the average hours spent per day for individual DM usage, the following formula was used: [(total number of hours spent on a weekday * 5) + (the total number of hours on a weekend day * 2)]/7. We used the average estimated time spent for individual DM usage, rather than relying solely on data from T0 or T4.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Brain structural development: global cortical surface area (CSA), striatum volume, cerebellum volume
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Structural MRI (FreeSurfer processing; Destrieux atlas for CSA, ASEG atlas for striatum and cerebellum volumes)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: These predefined outcomes included the global CSA and the volumes of the striatum and cerebellum. We defined the striatum by combining the volumes of the caudate nucleus, putamen, and accumbens. As for the cerebellum, we combined the volumes of both grey and white matter structures of the cerebellum. Both striatum and cerebellum volumes were adjusted for the total brain volumes.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Quadratic mixed-effect modelling (lmer in R) with random intercepts and slopes; DM usage x Time and DM usage x Time-squared interaction terms
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age at baseline (mean-centered), sex, SES (composite of household income, parental education, neighbourhood quality), cogPGS (polygenic scores for cognitive performance), 20 genetic principal components, ancestry, study sites
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We constructed a quadratic mixed-effect model to investigate the relationship between individual DM usage and brain structures over time. The model was adjusted for various factors: age at baseline, SES, cogPGS, 20 PCs, and sex assigned at birth as fixed effects, and study sites were included as random effects. The Bonferroni corrections were applied to control for Type-1 error. In total, we performed three individual DM usage models for three brain structures, resulting in a total of 18 tests. P < 0.003 was considered statistically significant.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social media usage x Time (linear interaction)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Cerebellum Volume: Social media usage x Time (linear): beta (SE) = 0.09 (0.008), p < 0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social media usage x Time-squared (quadratic interaction)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Cerebellum Volume: Social media usage x Time2: beta (SE) = -0.02 (0.003), p < 0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Playing video games x Time-squared (quadratic interaction)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Cerebellum Volume: Playing video games x Time2: beta (SE) = 0.01 (0.003), p < 0.001
Global cortical surface area: Playing video games: beta (SE) = -0.04 (0.01), p < 0.001
Global cortical surface area: Watching television: beta (SE) = -0.03 (0.008), p < 0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social media usage (accumulated 4-year effect, high vs low users)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Overall effect size of social media on cerebellum over 4 years
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: the accumulated effect of high-vs-low social media users on cerebellum volumes over 4 years was only beta = -0.03, which was considered insignificant

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: high social media usage was associated with a statistically significant change in the developmental trajectory of cerebellum volumes
3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: Although this is a longitudinal study with a large number of participants
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: the accumulated effect of high-vs-low social media users on cerebellum volumes over 4 years was only beta = -0.03, which was considered insignificant... the effect size observed for this association was smaller than our predefined threshold of 0.05. Moreover, in analysing the accumulated differences in cerebellum volumes over 4 years were also very small, which is likely not of relevance to the individual.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper uses a predefined threshold of 0.05 for meaningful effect sizes. Explicitly states that accumulated effects are too small to be individually relevant.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: These findings should be continued by longer follow-up, and more detailed documentation of DM usage, but is a cause for concern regarding the usage of social media in children and adolescents.
3H_Recommendations.notes: Paper expresses concern but does not issue specific clinical/policy recommendations.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion is notably cautious and transparent about effect sizes. It explicitly states the accumulated effect is very small (beta=-0.03), below their predefined threshold of meaningful effects (0.05), and likely not relevant to individuals. The paper distinguishes between statistically significant trajectory changes and practically meaningful effects. While it expresses concern about social media, it frames this as needing further longitudinal research rather than warranting intervention. This represents an unusually careful handling of small effects in this literature.
